**ABSTRACT****KEYWORDS** Testsmells are symptoms in the test code that indicate possible de-TestSmells, Refactoring, Open Source, Validation sign or implementation problems. Their presence, along with their**ACM** harmfulness,has already been demonstrated by previous researches.Elvys However,we do not know to what extent developers acknowledgedes, Alessandro Garcia, Baldoino Fonseca, and André Santos. 2020. Refac- the presenceof test smellsand how to refactorexistingcodeto toring Test Smells:A Perspectivefrom Open-SourceDevelopers.5thIn Brazilianeliminate them in practice. This study aims to assess open-source ’20),ACM,developers’ awareness about the existence of test smells and their [https://doi.org/10.1145/3425174.3425212](https://doi.org/10.1145/3425174.3425212)refactoring strategies. We conducted a mixed-methodstudy with two parts: (i) a survey with 73 experiencedopen-sourcedevelop- **1 INTRODUCTION**ers to assess their preferenceand motivationto choosebetween Automatedsoftware testing and the development of test scripts are10 differentsmellytestcodesamples,foundin 272 open-source frequent in the softwareindustry6[ A wide range of tools andprojects, and their refactored versions; and (ii) the submissionof frameworksfor these activities, whether in support of creating auto-50 pull requests to assess developers’ acceptance of the proposed matic tests or the complete automatic generation of these, is alreadyrefactorings. As a result, most surveyed developers preferred the available [3, 9]. Automatedtest suites with high internalqualityrefactored proposalfor78%oftheinvestigatedtestsmells,and the pull requests had an average acceptance of 75% among respon-facilitate maintenance activities, such as code understanding and regressiontesting. However, the test code’s development is tedious,dents. Additionally, we were able to provide empiric validation for error-prone, and requires a significant initial investment [1].literature-proposedrefactoring strategies. This study demonstrates Poorly designedtestcodeis frequentin practice1 ], andthethat although not always using the academic terminology, develop- symptoms that indicate possible design problems in the test codeers acknowledge both the negative impact of test smells presence are called bad test smells, or just test smells6 , 18[]. Theseand most of the literature’s proposals for their removal. are not the same as those defined by Fowler2 ] for[the production **CCS CONCEPTS**code. As they are based on test cases organization, implementation, and interaction with one another, their corresponding refactoring•**Software and**itsengineering→testingandde- operations generally differ from production code.**bugging;Empirical software validation .** 1As a motivating example, Figure 1 presents a code snippetex- ∗Thetracted from the core module of the AssertJ project, self-described atas “a set of strongly-typed assertions to use for unit testing with any test automation framework that is very close to plain English” . ThisACM contractortest intendstoverifya specificerroranditsproperties. nonexclusive,One can note the presence oftry/catchastructurein the test and to commands on lines 9 and 11 to handle the test outcome manually.SAST According to Peruma et al.11[], manually passing or failing a test© 2020 ACM 1[https://doi.org/10.1145/3425174.3425212](https://doi.org/10.1145/3425174.3425212)Original

SAST

depending on whether the production to a custom exception handling code in a test is expendable once the test framework already provides exception handling features to that end.

**Figure 1: Example of the Exception Handling test smell**

Later, we observeda test code refactoring new features and Java 8 lambda expressions that made the excep- tion test more transparentand eliminated manually dealing with errors, and test. Figure2 presentstherefactoring team, eliminating the fragility of the original design.

**Figure 2: Refactoring the Exception Handling test smell**

The provided example reflects what a test smell means: a symp- tom in the test code that may (or may not) indicate a design problem. The subsequent refactoring action demonstrated awareness of the design problem and implemented a coding strategy to eliminate it. Althoughnumerous previous studies have already demonstrated the presence of test smells in test suites of open-source projects, as well as their negative impact on code maintenance and understand- ing activities[ , 9 –11, 19], there is a lack of studies to assess the awareness of open-source developers about the existence of tests smells and their possible solutions. Thus, this study presents two empirical investigations that aim to answer the following research questions: 2Refactored

code throws an exception

2using both AssertJ

the previous passing or failing the performedby the

•**RQ****: To what extent test smells are relevant to open-**1 **source developers?** •**RQ****: To**whatextentopen-source2 **refactorings that remove test smells?**

The firstempiricalstudyis a survey opers to investigateRQby considering1 open-source developers aware of test smells and recognize smelly test code?”and “Do they know how to prevent or refactor a test smell?” . This study consisted of showing smelly test code snippets that we gathered from open-source projects, along with an alternate refac- tored version of the same test code. Then we asked developers to choose which version they preferred and justify their answers. The second empirical study aimed at answering tions such as“Do they acknowledge the existence of test smells in their projects?”consistsof submitting contributions (Pull Requests) with test coderefactoringsto active open-source whether the developers of such projects will accept the contribu- tions, along with considerations to any possible discussion topics that may emerge from the contributions. We have sent Pull Requests to 50 different open-source projects and, among respondents, we achieved a 75% acceptance rate, having most contributions being accepted with very little further discussions. In summary, this paper provides the following contributions:

•A survey with experienced open-source developers that as- sessed their impressions about test smells andneedfor ing strategies in Section 3; •An empirical study based on pull requests to better under-AssertJ stand the acceptance level of open-source developers to refac- torings of test smells in their projects, along with an empiri- cal validation of 10 literature-proposed refactoring strategies in Section 4;

**2 REFACTORING**TEST SMELLS We now present a brief explanation this study, the strategiesto eliminate snippets (simplified as toy examples). According to the test smell distributionpresented by Peruma et al. between the highest available ones on open-source projects:

**Figure 3: Conditional Test Logic**

**Conditional Test Logic:Defined by** smell occurs when the test depends on a condition. It is a dangerous design since a test method may result in a passed status without

developersaccept

withopen-sourcedevel- questionssuchas

**RQ**and ques-2

projectsand collect

refactor-

of the test smells we used in them and the relatedcode

<!-- Página 3 -->

Refactoring

ever having asserted a unit result. Palomba et10al.] propose refac- toring this test smell with an“ExtractMethod”[ 2 ] operation, which we believe to be more suitable in cases of nested loops and decisions. As our example uses a decision structure to verify a precondition, we added an assumption to the precondition, which will make the test to be ignoredif not met. Figure 3 presentsboth the original and the refactored versions. **Assertion Roulette:It is defined by van Deursen et al.] as** a collection of unexplained assertions in a single test method that makes it difficult to trace which exact assertion had a problem in the event of test failure. This test smell has the original refactoring suggestion of“Add Assertion Explanation” , and an alternative sug- gestion to break up the test into a suite“Single-ConditionofTests” proposed by Meszaros[ ]. As a single-conditiontestis actually a goodprogrammingpracticethatincreasescodecoverage,we opted to use this refactoring strategy. Figure 4 presents the original program with the Assertion Roulette and its refactored version.

**Figure 4: Assertion Roulette**

**Exception Handling:**According toPerumaetal. 11[ ], this smell occurs when the test manually handles both exceptions and test outcome and its frequency rivals with Assertion Roulette on Java projects.Figure5presentsthefirstcaseofthistestsmell, along with its refactoring, when properties after an exception must be verified.Figure6presentsthecase2oftheExceptionHan- dling test smell, that occurs when an exception message needs to be verified along with an alternate version that uses JUnit frame- work features.Figure7 presentscase3, alongwithan alternate refactored option, which happens when some final steps must be executed independentlyof the test result or the existence of any exceptions. Figure 8 presents case 4, which tests for a specific ex- ception being raised. The JUnit framework recommends the use of @Test(expected=MyException.class)annotation in this case.

**Sensitive Equality:van Deursen et al.18** ease to write equality checks using the a frequentalternativeto calculate sequence to compare to a literal string representing the expected value. However, these tests can depend on many irrelevant details, such as commas, quotes, and spaces. And whenevertoString() method for an object is changed, all related tests will start to fail. The proposed solution to this test smell is to replace the equality checks that use thetoString()method with real equality

[ ] state the agility and toString()method, being a result value and map it to a

the

using the Figure 9 presents an example refactored alternative.

RefactoringSAST

literals with no clear meaning (magic values). To refactor this test smell, the authors state that these numeric values can be replaced with constants or variables, thus providing a descriptive name for the value. This smell is presented in Figure 10. **Resource Optimism:According**to van Deursen et al.18[ this smell happenswhentest methodsmakeoptimisticassumptions about the existence or the state of external resources like files and databases.Such assumptions may cause non deterministic behavior on the outcome and can be refactored through theExternal Resource”, also defined before18[ ]. Figure 11 presents our example of Resource Optimism and the corresponding refactoring operation.

**Figure 12: Test Code Duplication**

**3.1 Planning** This survey aimed to analyze implementation preferences concern- ing the presence of test smells and their refactoring identification, from the viewpoint of open-sourcedevelopers, as statedRQby**:**1 **To**what extent test smells are relevant to open-source devel- **opers?** The generalideawastoaskdevelopersfortheirpreference betweentwo implementations of equivalent unit tests. One of these implementationswas always an original (smelly) code snippet, and the other one was a refactored alternative, following the refactoring principles of Murphy-Hill, Parnin, and Black [7].

**3.2 Settings** Toassemble the survey, we needed a list of test smells from open- source projects. As there is no public list of such smells, we had to **Figure 11: Resource Optimism**search and use a tool to analyze projects from public repositories. Althoughthe most comprehensive survey to the date3 ] lists[12 tools that handle test smells, the most recent and complete one is 18[**Test**Code Duplication:Also proposed van Deursen et al. the TSDetect [] tool proposed by Peruma et al.11 [ It detects 19 this test smell normally identifies classes that contain test methods differenttest smells and analyzes JUnit test cases, creating detailed with repeatedtestcodesteps.Typically, partsthatsetuptests execution logs. Using the GitHub search, we retrieved the projects fixtures are normally related to this test smell. The authors state list by classifyingJava projectsin descendingorder of stars and that, for cases where the duplicated code is in the same class, this executed the TSDetect [] in the listed results. According to the test smellcanbeeliminatedusing“Extract Method”, definedby tool executionlogs,weachieveda testsmelldistributionresult Fowler [2], as it is for normal code duplication. Figure 12 presents consistent with that reportedby Peruma et al.] with the first our example, as well as its refactored version. 272 results. The next sections present the empirical investigationswe per- formed in this study. The replicationpackagecontainingall the **3.3 Design** raw dataof bothinvestigationscanbefoundat thecompanion 3Intendingto have as many respondents as possible, we defined thewebsite. survey to take an average of 10 minutes of the developers’ time. This way, we searchedfor suitable test methodsthat (i) had few lines**3 SURVEY**WITHDEVELOPERS of code and could be displayed along with the refactored versionIn this section, we present our first empirical study performed with withoutthe need for scrolling the screen, and (ii) had an identifiableopen-source developers. test smell, albeit we did not stress if the code had any problem, nor 3[https://bit.ly/2EeL7Io](https://bit.ly/2EeL7Io)highlighted it in any way.
